text:
The Mandatory Nap Act of 2026 requires all adults between the ages of 25 and 65 to sleep for exactly 23 minutes between 1:00 and 3:00 PM on weekdays. Violations carry a fine of $47.

Here is why this is good policy.

Tired people make bad decisions. Studies consistently show that sleep deprivation impairs judgment, slows reaction time, and increases error rates across nearly every measurable task. The afternoon hours, specifically between 1 and 3 PM, correspond to a natural dip in human circadian rhythm. People are already drowsy. We are simply acknowledging what the body already wants.

The 23-minute duration is precise for a reason. Twenty minutes is enough to enter light sleep and recover alertness without crossing into deep sleep, which causes grogginess on waking. The extra three minutes accounts for the time most people spend lying down before actually falling asleep. This is evidence-based legislation.

Enforcement is straightforward. Employers would submit compliance reports quarterly. Spot checks would involve wearable sleep monitors, which most people already own. The $47 fine was chosen because it is annoying enough to motivate compliance but not ruinous enough to generate public outrage. Behavioral economists call this the sweet spot.

Economic benefits are real. A well-rested workforce produces more output per hour. Medical costs decline when people sleep adequately. Fewer accidents happen. Insurance companies would likely support this bill once the actuarial tables are updated.

Critics will call this government overreach. But we already regulate seatbelts, food safety, and building codes. Protecting people from their own exhaustion is no different. The Mandatory Nap Act is not ridiculous. It is simply ahead of its time.
